text: My thesis is that if we start with the supposition that there is only one primal stuff or material in the world, a stuff of which everything is composed, and if we call that stuff 'pure experience,' then knowing can easily be explained as a particular sort of relation towards one another into which portions of pure experience may enter.
author: William James
locator: Essays in Radical Empiricism, essay I "Does 'Consciousness' Exist?", section I (opening of "My thesis")
out_pulls_note: Out-pulls the gloss: James names the single 'primal stuff' / 'pure experience' from which both knower and known are arrangements, giving the actual neutral-monist move rather than just asserting it.
search_notes: Found in Project Gutenberg #32547 (James, Essays in Radical Empiricism). Passage opens section I of 'Does "Consciousness" Exist?' (1904). Verbatim substring confirmed after normalising curly quotes/whitespace. Span deliberately ends at 'may enter.' to avoid the footnote marker [5] a few words later; no Gutenberg italic underscores within the span. Author guidance preferred James over Russell when unsure; James used. High confidence, no misattribution risk (well-documented founding essay).
